speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I have a tendency also to stutter when I-- when I get nervous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Steinberg, you were telling me about the children who were coming from Germany as refugees. In what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was about 1938 or '39, I can't recall exactly, but it was-- was very close to-- between '39, so a year before the war [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they coming without their parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't recall that either, because they were-- they must have-- they must have taken them out, they must have-- maybe the parents-- I don't remember that, no. Because I know they were children. There were different kind of children there of different-- especially Germany, there were-- when they were chased out from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were telling us that you were preoccupied with orphanages.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, not preoccupied-- I used to like to go to-- to the orphanages and feel like-- I didn't do it continuously, but I used to like to go to the orphanages [INAUDIBLE] to the [INAUDIBLE] old people. I used to-- I used to feel, for some reason, probably the closest there. I felt like a-- a compassion for these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did actually anti-Semitism affect you personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably beginning of '39, when I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was beginning to feel that I am involved as a Jew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm involved in the situation myself, [INAUDIBLE] because I'm beginning-- I was 14 years old already, going to be 14-- I-- I became to be 14 years old, no. And my-- the talk was going around, the way people were talking on the radio, no. I saw-- I saw people talking about it, no, that-- that something is going on in Germany. They were showing pictures of Hitler [INAUDIBLE]. They were showing they way he was expressing his [? word, ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And the way he was teaching his people how to hurt other people. I saw the kristall-- I saw the-- when they were breaking windows. And the-- in the papers, no, I saw the way they were beating people, they were cutting beards for people, no. The way they were burning the books on the street, I saw. I saw different things, in the paper. So it gave me some kind of realization, some kind of-- some kind of an outlook. That's what might happen to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did it happen to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe how that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was early morning, you know. The bombs-- were tremendous-- the bombs. They-- one bomb we heard as big-- just roaring off the airplanes. And we lived in-- on the second floor. And-- and each house had a some kind of like a-- a master in charge of the building, you know. And they-- they screamed, get down, get down, come out, we're being attacked. And we all went down to the first floor, the ground floor. We went into the first apartment there. They said, if you're downstairs, you know, nothing is going to happen to you. You're safe. How foolish they were. And we stayed there until about three or four houses from us the houses were completely demolished. All the people were killed alive. A whole huge building. We were on Number 21 on [INAUDIBLE], which was our street number. I think it was nine or seven, the number, that was hit. Another number was hit there-- I think it was 11 I think it was hit too. So it was very close to us, you know. And the bombs were huge bombs. And-- and-- and-- and I-- I remember-- I remember, well, we were downstairs, you know, and-- and the streets were empty. And only children were like runners. They were-- they were-- they were taking children as runners-- go here, go here, find out what's going on next block. Find out here. So I think I did some running. My brother did some running, I did some running. Some kind of-- of orders to do. Something to do-- just to find out what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see when you were running?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw people dead. We saw people-- about a few hours later, it started smelling, that it-- it was very dusty, from the bombs, no. And-- And we saw people-- fleshes-- flesh of people laying on the street, no, from the-- from the people got killed from the street from the-- from the bombs, no. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we-- well, it was-- it was-- it was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. Well, I was a young kid, and I was very frightened. Bombs were the first experience I ever had, no. I never heard bombs, no. Of course I was afraid. And for a long time, afterward, no, any kind of explosion, or any kind of sound of a big loud noise, no, used to affect my ears, no, from those bombs. Even when I was in the-- in the camps later on, there was still like a shimmering noise in my ears, hearing from those bombs, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the bombing was over, what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to the house. We went back there. We came out. We tried to help. We went-- first we went to houses, no, where they were bombed, to see what's going on there. How many people got killed, no. Oh, they were screaming, you know. They were hollering. People were hollering, oh, this one got killed, this one got killed, this one got killed, no. People were crying. People were screaming, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was-- and I was very thankful that it didn't happen to us, you know. Not-- I was-- but not even thinking that somebody else got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know who was bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, did we-- we heard the Germans. The Germans attacked us. We saw-- we-- we saw the Polish soldiers that going on the streets, no. We saw people with-- with-- with the arm bands on the street, no, like these-- civil defense going on the streets, no. We saw mostly-- a lot of soldiers walking on the street, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Poland mobilize?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland mobilized right away, and they called everybody. They called, no, who was a certain age to go in the army. They took most-- most the people from our building. They took, no, they took people who were in the army before, in the reserve. They mobilized them right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews, too. Yeah, most-- all Jews were taken. Most of the Jews who were in the army before, and to a certain age, you know, were taken to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, then, right away, the shortage of bread, shortage in food, no. We stayed in line for hours. We stayed in lines, no, for food, for bread, no. And we-- we-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was very hard. It was very hard right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first see Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: About six or seven days later, maybe even less than that. They marched in. I saw the huge-- huge tanks, I saw. Unusual tanks. I never saw this kind of tanks in my life, no. They were so dusty. The people were so dusty, you know. And the-- they were-- they came in and they had-- the-- the-- the streets were tumbling the streets, making noise from these big tanks, no. Then, they-- after the tanks came in, then I saw-- there was-- infantry came in. And I saw the people-- soldiers on horses, wagons, cars, trucks, no, whole convoys. Hundreds, thousands of soldiers came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Polish population behave, the non-Jewish Polish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the time-- well, they were in very bad conditions, so they didn't do nothing about it. Quite a few people, quite a few, right away volunteered, because quite a few non-Jews-- non-Jews were Germans, half Germans. They used to call them volksdeutsche. So they volunteered. They greeted the Germans on the street, and with their hand showing-- heil Hitler-- they were screaming, you know. Of course, I-- we were-- I didn't know exactly. I thought, to me, they looked like good people, the Germans, because they look like me, like-- like regular human beings, you know. I didn't feel they were involved. They were just taken to the army to fight the war. And I went out, no, and I-- sold bagels to them, made money. My uncle had a bagel-- bagel factory. And me and my brother went out, you know, and he gave me bagels on consignment, bags of bagels. And I sold them to these soldiers. I lift-- I lift myself up on the tanks, sold them the bagels and gave them-- they gave me money. It was a big thing, you know. I thought-- I never saw that before. I thought maybe they are better than the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they behave?

speaker: interviewee
text: They behaved very nice. These people behaved very nice, the Germans, you know. They came in. They were regular soldiers, you know. Wehrmacht, they-- they were called, no. There were-- there were some bad ones, of course, no. Sometimes, no, did something. But what I seen-- what I seen, they weren't [INAUDIBLE], no. They had their own troubles, you know, fighting a war and trying to survive, no. So I didn't see them to be bad when-- when they arrived to Poland. Of course, no, there's something-- but I never knew-- there's another German came in. They had little tiny SSs. They were the ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first saw them?

speaker: interviewee
text: About six days later, five days later, a group of people came in. They were all dressed in black and brown with big-- big boots, brown boots, black boots, no. They looked different than the regular soldiers, no. They looked mean. They looked something-- I would take something away from them that they-- they felt I-- that I was against them. And they, right away, I saw, the first thing they did, no, is catching Jews with long beards on the streets. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they put pork, and rubbed their face in pork. And they used to laugh. They-- they took scissors, unsharpened scissors, and cut their beards, and pulled their beards, no. These pious people.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were trying to shave them. And they were screaming, where's your god? Your god is supposed to be here. How come he's not here? I'm doing those things to you. Where is he? Tell me, where is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: These poor people, Jews that before the war I used to admire them, even though I was not religious, no, but I still had respect for anybody who has any kind of religion, I had respect for, because they have a right to have their religions, no. Everybody has got a right. I felt, when I was even young. And they-- they used to put them on the ground and beat them. And these people had that-- they're called tzitzis, you know, these-- hanging down from their clothes, you know. They used to pull down, take them-- show them with the [INAUDIBLE] what they call it in Jewish, no. They wear a little- under their clothes, they used to wear some kind of, like a little tallith, no. And they used to take off your jacket. Show the world what you are. You're very pious, no. Show me your payos. And they used to cut it off, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this with your own eyes?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my own eyes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you first saw such an attack, how did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt-- I felt sorry for these people. I never knew that people of a high cultural society, which I learned in school, could-- could commit this kind of atrocities, this kind of shameful thing, to other people. I always heard, when I was in school, that good piano players and good musicians were Germans. Good learners. People, before the war, used to go to Germany to become doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here murderers came, murderers, no, and doing this kind of atrocities to other people. So I was kind of wondering, how can they? And why are they doing that? How frightened were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much. I was afraid, when am I-- when they're going to come to me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it happen to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not now, not then, but eventually. Eventually, I was in line, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was-- he didn't have no work. And, of course, no, right away-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my father was-- was very-- he didn't know what to do, because there was no work, you know. And-- and right away they performed-- they had-- they-- they blocked off the city. They blocked off-- made a ghetto out of it. And they took all the Jews from other sections, who lived in non-Jewish neighborhoods, no, and they put them in to the Jewish neighborhood, too. In other words, to be together, all the Jews to be in one section. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And-- and I never heard the word ghetto. And that's what happened, the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they gave us a little piece of bread. And whoever-- it was-- it was still after the war-- before the war just started, you know, so there-- there were some supplies left in certain stores, no. And if you had jewelry, and if you had any kind of precious metals, no, precious diamonds, no, you could exchange for commodities, for these commodities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you get out of the ghetto to do some shopping?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. It was impossible. There were-- they had guards there. They had-- They guarded us like you would be-- like you-- like you would be the most-- the most precious thing in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto surrounded with a fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had fences, it had chicken wires, and had-- and it had all these things, no. Not to be [INAUDIBLE]. It had sentries, with police-- with-- with police outside, no. And there's no way to go out. The only way you could go out, no, through a gate, is to go to work. And we had to be counted before the work, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the ghetto organized?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was organized by the Germans, no, and run by the Judenrat. Judenrat is a-- was a certain kind of-- it was done by the Jews, itself, no. They took some intelligent Jews, no. They made him a leader. They made someone be a treasurer. They made somebody be a doctor. They made somebody to be this, until-- and these people run the ghetto. It was called the Judenrat. Jews control its own Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I never paid attention. I was more hungry than-- than looking at their-- their-- their behavior. I didn't worry about their behavior. I knew it was, bad because whoever-- in any kind of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anybody, had any kind of [? protectia, ?] it's called. [? Protectia ?] means to wear protection-- means any kind of-- of knowledge, no, became big, and anybody who-- who was very weak, became small. Some people became very big, you know, and they had big positions there, like in the-- in the Judenrat, no. And some people, who didn't have no power, were small. Didn't have-- [INAUDIBLE] plain people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you have to do in the ghetto? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto-- we didn't do nothing. The ghetto we didn't do nothing. They just-- you were very fortunate-- they came to the ghetto. The Judenrat used to give us-- used to catch people on the street, no. The Germans would catch people on the street, and take us out on the non-Jewish section to work. They used to come and-- give me 200 Jews, give me 300 Jews, give me 500 Jews, no. And that's how we used to go in to work. We had certain details, you know, we used to fulfill a day. Sometimes we worked at the airport. Sometimes we worked at garbage. Sometime we worked on-- on the streets, fix the streets. Sometime we worked on on-- on the park, cleaning up, you know. It was very strenuous and very dirty work we committed. We-- we-- we-- we did. I'm sorry. We did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and your brothers work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, My brother was very young, my brother, those days, no. But I was, sometimes, since I was a little bit larger in size, no. I could've get by with an older age. So they thought I was older, you know, and occasionally I was taken out, no, for a detail work, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father was never taken out, no. I don't think so-- he taken out but--

speaker: interviewer
text: How come?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember he-- I remember they just-- they just stayed home. Stayed home. Maybe he'd fix somebody's pants, or sometimes, no, and made a few cents, no, or fix somebody shirt, no, in the-- from the-- in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an advantage of going out to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Big advantage. Why? It was big-- there was good and bad. If we went to the Germans-- the SS-- to work, no, you knew for sure you were going to come back beaten up. Because this was the-- they'd take us and beat the heck out of us, no. If we went to the other, to the wehrmacht to work, no, or the other soldiers, no, somebody else, maybe to civilian people, we were able to ask to help us, no. So they used to give a piece of bread, the non-Jewish people, or the-- or the Germans, no. They give us bread, no, maybe some piece of [INAUDIBLE], no, sugar, no. We used to steal it and smuggle it into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Polish non-Jewish population behave at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't get the-- I didn't get-- only one incident I had one time with a non-Polish Polish person. I asked, one time, a Polish fella-- help me, give me some bread to my father and my brothers, please give me some bread, no. Bring me something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, bring me your silver, and I'll give it to you. Bring me your gold that your mother had. He never knew me before. I said, I haven't got any.

speaker: interviewee
text: He felt all Jews are wealthy, all Jews are very rich, no, and they contain these items, no. I didn't have. Maybe I had a silver spoon or-- or something, no. Because, you know my father was not a materialistic person to believe in all these silver or gold. He was a working man, no, and we didn't believe in those kinds of things, no. We were very-- we lived from day to day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in the ghetto until 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the ghetto formed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was formed right after the-- after the Germans came in. When-- when they told us to wear armbands with patches on the back, yellow patches on the back, no. And I'm sure the guy who made the-- the armbands made something. We used to go out and buy these things, no. And then my father said, sew them on, because they couldn't be creased. We couldn't-- these armbands could not, couldn't wear them being creased. They-- they had to be perfect so they'll be visible. That the Star of David should be very visible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This probably was 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the ghetto ended in '42, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the selection happened. But something happened before. I want to bring something up, no. I was very hungry in the ghetto. Things were-- people were dying on the street, you know. The little cart that picked up the dead people was busy. People were just laying on the street. It was very bad, three- no, 2 and 1/2 years after the war. And Jewish people are very, very [? honored ?] people. It-- they became-- the rich people became poor people, and they were begging, too. Everybody is begging for food. I was sitting once in the-- in the gutter near my house. A big truck, huge truck, drove in to the ghetto. I was very, very afraid. And he caught me. He screamed, I need 26 Jews. This man was a very tall man. I was about-- I know-- I was a child, 14 years old, no. But I maybe I looked like 15 or 16, because he grabbed me. So he figured I'm, you know, an adult. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he grabbed me. When he grabbed me, he put me on the truck. Sit right there and shut up, he told me in German. And he went out and caught 25 more, and took them all in the truck, you know. And shut up. Don't say nothing, you dirty Jews, you know. Everybody heard it, no, everybody-- and the other Germans, who heard it, no, oh, this guy is good. Oh, he hates Jews. He hates-- you know. Good. Good. Give them. He had a big huge-- he held a big whip in his hand. I went in the truck, you know. I'll never forget for the rest of my life, no, and I said to myself, no, [SPEAKING HEBREW]. That's it, my end of life. It's starts from the Kaddish. I figured, that's it, my dad's not going to know happened to me. My younger brother is not going to know. Nobody's going to know about it. And I sat on the corner waiting for the next move, what's going to be. Until he had 26 people, he went ahead, no. The cover from the truck was a canvas cover the locks on the bottom. I'll never forget that. And he locked it up. And the Germans used some kind of coal, or wood, on the trucks, no. It made inside very smoky. Oh, I said, my god, I'm going to choke to death. And I couldn't breathe, and I was screaming. I couldn't breath, no, because the exhaust from the truck was terrible. And I heard him, I heard it from him-- from the [INAUDIBLE] plastic flaps from the front. So I looked out, and I saw when he would-- approach the gate from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he approached, no, the sentry near the-- the entrance from the ghetto, asked him, what you got there? He says, I got 26 damned Jews, no, dirty Jews, no. They're going to be working their-- their behind off. And the German-- near the gates, OK, good luck. I'm glad.

speaker: interviewee
text: He drove us out. We drove about a mile, we drove, no. And they put us-- they took us to a very-- in a-- to a place, no, where not many people were congregating. He opened up the flap and he said, come out. Come out. Everybody out. He said, OK, men, you are in good hands. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I came to help you. My name is Sergeant Schreider. [INAUDIBLE] says, no, we didn't believe him. I couldn't believe a German would be as good like this, no. What is his motive?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is his reason, I said, no. Maybe he's got something behind his sleeve that he's going to do something, a joke, you know, and he's going to kill us, no. And we wouldn't-- nobody was going to know about it. I was so frightened. I was crying. Everybody was crying. The 26 Jews were crying, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he said, what can I do to prove myself to you that I'm good for you? He went in the front seat, no. He took out bread, large loaves of bread, and gave each a piece-- a loaf of bread. We ate like it's going out of style. He says, I've got something else for you, fellas. I got some sausage for you. I know you're Jewish, no, but you need Polish sausage. Gave us sausage, and then he said, who smokes cigarettes? He gave us cigarettes, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we did [INAUDIBLE]. And we all ate, in the-- sat in the truck, you know. Go back in the truck and eat it, no. And if anybody should see [? what's ?] going on here. And he drove the truck himself, no. He didn't have no chauffeur. [INAUDIBLE] remember. And afterwards, we all sat down. We went back to the truck, and he locked it again together, the flap, you know, and he drove in to the base where he was stationed. He was stationed to a place-- I only remembered of the [INAUDIBLE] on the [INAUDIBLE] street, it's called, no. The base was called the [INAUDIBLE]. And we walked in there-- everybody out. The guard right away let us in, because he was in charge of the base. He was a-- he was like a master sergeant, or something, no. And it was-- it was called ober-- oberfeldwebel, I'll never forget that, oberfeldwebel. It means like a-- like a master sergeant, in charge, you know. We went in inside. Everybody out, no. Went outside. We made-- we lined up, three to a line. OK, so you're going to do this job. You're going to do this job. You're going to do this job, no, OK. Anything you want to know, anything you want to do, don't go to nobody, come to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what he said. I said, what am I supposed to do. He says, no, you're going to be in charge of flowers. To do what? Well, if you find a little weed, you know, peel it up, no. If it takes water, water it, no. If you find a bud, cut it, no. If you find anything that's not necessary to flowers, no, save it, no. Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp. In this-- in this-- in this army camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had flowers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had little flowers there, yeah. Near the-- near the barracks, you know, there were little flowers. Yeah, in the army, you know, this particular army base had little-- the guys-- the soldiers put in flowers, you know, outside, no. They grow and they had little vegetables, no. Army guys do that, no. I learned that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop again for a few minutes.
